_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 1
questions.prompt: Which block runs whether try succeeds or fails?
questions.explanation: A finally block runs after the try and any matching catch, making it suitable for cleanup that both paths require.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 2
questions.prompt: What is wrong with an empty catch block?
choices: It hides the failure and its evidence
It converts the error to text
questions.explanation: The program silently continues, so neither the user nor the developer can see that save failed or why it failed.

questions.title: Trace Error Handling
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: JSON.parse throws before the Loaded message, so catch provides a controlled response. The resulting output is 'Could not read that JSON'.

questions.title: Error Handling practice 5
questions.prompt: What is the problem with `try { riskyWork(); } catch (error) {}`?
choices: The empty catch swallows the failure, so the program looks like it succeeded
An empty catch block is a syntax error
catch cannot take a parameter named error
try requires a finally block to run
questions.explanation: An empty catch discards the error, so a failed `riskyWork()` leaves no trace and the code continues as if all was well. The better version at least logs it and re-throws so a caller who can handle it still gets the chance. An empty catch is legal syntax, `error` is a fine parameter name, and `finally` is optional.

questions.title: Error Handling concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Catch narrowly"?
choices: Do not wrap a whole application in one broad try block. Keep the protected section close to the operation whose failure you understand, so unrelated programming mistakes are not mislabeled as expected input problems.
questions.explanation: Use `try` around an operation that can fail and `catch` to respond to the resulting Error. Inspect the error, give the user a useful response, and avoid empty catch blocks that hide the cause. That reasoning supports the answer: Do not wrap a whole application in one broad try block. Keep the protected section close to the operation whose failure you understand, so unrelated programming mistakes are not mislabeled as expected input problems.

questions.title: Error Handling concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Error Handling practice?
choices: Protect only operations you expect can fail.
It converts the error to text
questions.explanation: Use `try` around an operation that can fail and `catch` to respond to the resulting Error. Inspect the error, give the user a useful response, and avoid empty catch blocks that hide the cause. That reasoning supports the answer: Protect only operations you expect can fail.

questions.title: Error Handling concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Error Handling code?
choices: Read the Error name and message.
questions.explanation: Use `try` around an operation that can fail and `catch` to respond to the resulting Error. Inspect the error, give the user a useful response, and avoid empty catch blocks that hide the cause. That reasoning supports the answer: Read the Error name and message.

questions.title: Error Handling concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Error Handling mistake?
choices: Give users a specific recovery path.
It converts the error to text
questions.explanation: Use `try` around an operation that can fail and `catch` to respond to the resulting Error. Inspect the error, give the user a useful response, and avoid empty catch blocks that hide the cause. That reasoning supports the answer: Give users a specific recovery path.

questions.title: Error Handling concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Error Handling. Which advice is correct?
choices: Rethrow errors the current layer cannot handle.
questions.explanation: Use `try` around an operation that can fail and `catch` to respond to the resulting Error. Inspect the error, give the user a useful response, and avoid empty catch blocks that hide the cause. That reasoning supports the answer: Rethrow errors the current layer cannot handle.

questions.title: Error Handling concept check 6
questions.prompt: What is the main idea behind Error Handling?
choices: It converts the error to text
Handle expected failures while keeping real programming mistakes visible.
questions.explanation: Use `try` around an operation that can fail and `catch` to respond to the resulting Error. Inspect the error, give the user a useful response, and avoid empty catch blocks that hide the cause. That reasoning supports the answer: Handle expected failures while keeping real programming mistakes visible.
